sentences.base_language: My recipe for paella
sentences.target_language: Mi receta para la paella
sentences.grammarNote:
**Mi receta** = my recipe — **mi** (my) is an unstressed possessive adjective: mi, tu, su, nuestro, vuestro, su. No accent mark on **mi** (possessive) vs. **mí** (stressed pronoun: 'me').
**Para la paella** — **para** here expresses purpose/topic ('for paella'). **La paella** takes the definite article when referring to the dish as a known concept.

sentences.base_language: One of my favorite foods is paella
sentences.target_language: Uno de mis alimentos favoritos es la paella
sentences.grammarNote:
**Uno de mis... favoritos** = one of my favorites — **uno de + plural noun** is a common superlative-like construction. Note: **mis** (plural possessive) because **alimentos** is plural.
**Alimentos** (foods/foodstuffs) is a more formal/nutritional word than **comidas** (meals/foods). From Latin *alimentum* (nourishment), related to *alere* (to nourish) — also the root of English 'aliment.'

sentences.base_language: Traditionally, paella is made with sea food but My recipe is vegetarian
sentences.target_language: Tradicionalmente, la paella se hace con mariscos pero mi receta es vegetariana
sentences.grammarNote: "Se hace" is the passive reflexive construction — 'se' + third person verb. Very common in Spanish for describing how things are done in general.

sentences.base_language: First I cut half a yellow onion,
sentences.target_language: Primero corto media cebolla amarilla,
sentences.grammarNote:
**Media cebolla** = half an onion — **medio/media** (half) agrees in gender with the noun: **medio limón**, **media cebolla**, **medio kilo**.
**Corto** = I cut (yo form of **cortar**, a regular -ar verb). Note: **cortar** is the general word; **picar** means to chop finely; **rebanar** means to slice.

sentences.base_language: I mince four cloves of garlic,
sentences.target_language: Pico cuatro dientes de ajo,
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pico** = I mince/chop finely (yo form of **picar**). **Picar** also means to itch, to sting, or to peck — context clarifies.
**Dientes de ajo** = cloves of garlic — literally 'teeth of garlic.' **Diente** (tooth) is used because garlic cloves resemble teeth. From Latin *dens, dentis* — also root of English 'dental.'

sentences.base_language: And I chop 2 small carrots and a bell pepper
sentences.target_language: Y corto 2 zanahorias pequeñas y un pimiento
sentences.grammarNote:
**Zanahorias** (carrots) — this word has Arabic roots: from Arabic *isfannāriyya* via Old Spanish. Many Spanish vegetable names entered through the Moorish period.
**Pimiento** (bell pepper) vs. **pimienta** (black pepper spice) — an easy pair to confuse! **Pimiento** is the vegetable; **pimienta** is the ground spice.

sentences.base_language: Everything is cooked in one pan so I start heating up some oil on the stove
sentences.target_language: Todo se cocina en una sola sartén, así que empiezo calentando un poco de aceite en la estufa
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se cocina** — passive reflexive: 'everything is cooked' / 'everything cooks itself.' The passive reflexive with **se** is the standard way to express passive voice in Spanish.
**Empiezo calentando** = I start by heating — **empezar + gerund** (verb ending in **-ando/-iendo**) describes how something begins. Compare: **empezar a + infinitive** (start to do) vs. **empezar + gerundio** (start by doing).

sentences.base_language: Cook the onion and garlic until the onion is translucent
sentences.target_language: Cocino la cebolla y el ajo hasta que la cebolla esté translúcida
sentences.grammarNote: "Hasta que...esté" requires the subjunctive because the outcome is not yet certain at the time of cooking.

sentences.base_language: Then you add the carrots, bell pepper, and a can of artichokes
sentences.target_language: Luego agregas las zanahorias, el pimiento y una lata de alcachofas
sentences.grammarNote:
**Agregas** = you add — the **tú** form used informally to give recipe instructions (like 'you add' in English cooking). This is the informal/conversational recipe register.
**Alcachofa** (artichoke) comes from Arabic *al-kharshūf* — one of the clearest examples of Arabic vocabulary in Spanish. The word entered English via Spanish/Italian: artichoke.

sentences.base_language: After ten minutes add half a can of tomato paste
sentences.target_language: Después de diez minutos agrego media lata de pasta de tomate
sentences.grammarNote:
**Después de + time expression** = after (a period of time). **Después de diez minutos** = after ten minutes.
**Media lata** = half a can — **media** (feminine) agrees with **lata** (can, feminine). **Pasta de tomate** = tomato paste; **salsa de tomate** = tomato sauce — the two are different concentrations.

sentences.base_language: Season with black pepper, paprika, salt, and just a pinch of saffron (optional)
sentences.target_language: Sazono con pimienta negra, pimentón, sal y solo una pizca de azafrán (opcional)
sentences.grammarNote:
**Sazono** = I season (yo form of **sazonar**, to season). **Sazón** (seasoning/flavor) is also used as a noun; in Latin American cooking, **sazón** is a popular spice blend.
**Azafrán** (saffron) is from Arabic *az-za'farān* — one of the world's most expensive spices, key to authentic paella's color. **Una pizca** = a pinch — literally 'a pinch (between fingers),' from **pellizcar** (to pinch).

sentences.base_language: Evenly cover
sentences.target_language: Cubro uniformemente
sentences.grammarNote:
**Cubro** = I cover (yo form of **cubrir**, a regular -ir verb). Past participle: **cubierto** (irregular) — used in compound tenses: **he cubierto** (I have covered).
**Uniformemente** = evenly/uniformly — adverbs in Spanish are formed by adding **-mente** to the feminine form of the adjective: **uniforme → uniformemente**, **rápida → rápidamente**, **fácil → fácilmente** (no gender change needed for -mente when adjective is invariable).

sentences.base_language: After all that mixes in well add two and a half cups of vegeatable broth
sentences.target_language: Después de que todo eso se mezcle bien, agrego dos tazas y media de caldo de verduras
sentences.grammarNote:
**Después de que todo eso se mezcle** — **después de que + subjunctive** is required when the action refers to a future or unresolved event. Compare: **después de mezclar** (after mixing — same subject, use infinitive) vs. **después de que se mezcle** (after it mixes — different subject, use subjunctive).
**Caldo de verduras** = vegetable broth/stock. **Caldo** from Latin *calidus* (warm/hot) — same root as **caliente** (hot) and English 'caloric.'

sentences.base_language: If you want it to be more like traditional paella you would add chicken broth
sentences.target_language: Si quieres que sea más como la paella tradicional, agregarías caldo de pollo
sentences.grammarNote:
**Si quieres que sea** — **querer que + subjunctive**: wanting someone/something to do something triggers the subjunctive: **sea** (present subjunctive of **ser**).
**Agregarías** is the conditional tense: 'you would add.' The conditional of regular -ar verbs: infinitive + -ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían. Used for hypothetical actions.

sentences.base_language: Bring the broth up to a boil then add bomba rice evenly
sentences.target_language: Llevo el caldo a ebullición y luego agrego el arroz bomba de manera uniforme
sentences.grammarNote:
**Llevo el caldo a ebullición** = I bring the broth to a boil. **Ebullición** (boiling) from Latin *ebullitio* — related to **burbuja** (bubble). Also: **hervir** (to boil): **llevo a ebullición** and **dejo hervir** (let it boil) are interchangeable.
**De manera uniforme** = evenly/uniformly — an alternative to the adverb **uniformemente**. **De manera + adjective** is a common Spanish construction: de manera uniforme, de manera rápida, de manera eficiente.

sentences.base_language: Cook on high for 5 minutes
sentences.target_language: Cocino a fuego alto durante 5 minutos
sentences.grammarNote:
**A fuego alto** = on high heat. The full heat scale: **a fuego alto** (high), **a fuego medio** (medium), **a fuego bajo/lento** (low/simmer).
**Durante** = for (duration of time) — used with time expressions to show how long something lasts: **durante cinco minutos** (for five minutes), **durante dos horas** (for two hours). Compare **por**, which is also used for duration but more loosely.

sentences.base_language: Cook on medium for 5 minutes
sentences.target_language: Cocino a fuego medio durante 5 minutos
sentences.grammarNote:
**Fuego medio** = medium heat. **Medio/media** has several meanings: half (**medio kilo**), medium/average (**de talla media** = medium-sized), or the middle (**en medio de** = in the middle of).
**Durante 5 minutos** — note that Spanish often writes out numbers in text but numerals are common in recipes and instructions.

sentences.base_language: Let the paella cook for another fifteen minutes on low (gently simmer)
sentences.target_language: Dejo que la paella se cocine otros quince minutos a fuego bajo (hervor suave)
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dejo que la paella se cocine** = I let the paella cook — **dejar que + subjunctive**: when the subject of the letting and the cooking are different, subjunctive is required: **se cocine** (present subjunctive of **cocinar** reflexive).
**Hervor suave** = gentle simmer — **hervor** is the noun form of **hervir** (to boil). **Suave** = gentle/soft/mild, from Latin *suavis* (sweet/pleasant) — also root of English 'suave.'

sentences.base_language: You can add shrimp at this stage if you want some sort of meat
sentences.target_language: Puedes agregar camarones en esta etapa si quieres algún tipo de carne
sentences.grammarNote:
**Puedes agregar** = you can add — **poder + infinitive** expresses ability or possibility. **Poder** is o→ue stem-changing: puedo, puedes, puede, podemos, podéis, pueden.
**Camarones** (shrimp) = the Latin American term; in Spain: **gambas** or **langostinos** (larger prawns). **Algún tipo de** = some sort/kind of — **algún** is the shortened form of **alguno** before masculine singular nouns.

sentences.base_language: I like to burn it a little at the end by going back to high for one minute
sentences.target_language: Me gusta quemarlo un poco al final volviendo a fuego alto por un minuto
sentences.grammarNote:
**Me gusta quemarlo** = I like to burn it — gustar-type construction: the action (quemarlo) is the grammatical subject, and **me** is the indirect object pronoun ('it pleases me to burn it').
**Al final** = **a + el final** — mandatory contraction. Means 'at the end.'
**Volviendo** is the gerund of **volver** (to return/go back): 'by returning to high heat.' The gerund in Spanish (ending in **-ando/-iendo**) is used to describe a simultaneous action.

sentences.base_language: Take off the stove, cover and let rest for ten minutes
sentences.target_language: Retiro del fuego, tapo y dejo reposar durante diez minutos
sentences.grammarNote:
**Del fuego** = **de + el fuego** — mandatory contraction. 'Off the fire/heat.'
**Dejo reposar** = I let it rest — **dejar + infinitive** (same subject implied): 'let rest.' When the object is implied, no subjunctive is needed; the infinitive follows directly.
**Reposar** (to rest) is also used for people taking a rest after eating: **necesito reposar** (I need to rest).

sentences.base_language: And that's it, a pretty good paella recipe for 4 people
sentences.target_language: Y eso es todo, una receta de paella bastante buena para 4 personas
sentences.grammarNote:
**Eso es todo** = that's it / that's all — **eso** is the neuter demonstrative pronoun (no gender), used for a concept or situation rather than a specific object.
**Bastante buena** = pretty good / quite good — **bastante** (quite/fairly/enough) is an adverb modifying the adjective. **Bastante** from Latin *bastare* (to suffice) — 'enough to be good.'

sentences.base_language: Let me know if you like it!
sentences.target_language: ¡Dime si te gusta!
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dime** = tell me — imperative of **decir** + **me** attached (see affirmative command pronoun attachment).
**Si te gusta** = if you like it — **gustar** construction: **te** (indirect object, 'to you') + **gusta** (third-person singular, because the implied subject 'it' is singular). Note **si** (if) without accent, vs. **sí** (yes) with accent.
